document.diagnosis: The main bottleneck is converting candidate coverage into a correct final answer, not merely increasing calls. In Iteration 14, all three repair systems reached 7/12 exact with 0.50 weakest-family accuracy, while five-base plurality reached only 2/12 despite a 7/12 candidate oracle. The nine-answer bank raised oracle coverage to 9/12, but its one-review system still finished at 7/12. Extra parallel reviewers showed no benefit: five-bank one-review and three-review repair each made five helpful and zero harmful corrections and both scored 7/12, despite the latter using two more review calls per activated case. Blind regeneration introduced two otherwise-absent correct answers and raised coverage from 7/12 to 9/12, but augmented plurality converted only one wrong base result and finished 3/12 with 0.00 sequence accuracy. This supports a paired one-versus-three-review test on the shared nine-answer bank, continued measurement of regeneration coverage, and a future mechanism that reviews generated candidates instead of relying on plurality to surface them.
extensions_requested: Add one generation-then-falsification primitive: three blind domain-neutral regenerators augment the raw candidate bank, after which one falsifying repair reviewer sees shuffled unique augmented candidates with frequencies hidden. A valid review answer wins; otherwise fall back to the original base plurality. Track regeneration coverage and reviewer helpful/harmful interventions separately.
most_important_evidence: Iteration 14 repair systems tied at 7/12 exact and 0.50 weakest-family accuracy; each recorded five helpful and zero harmful interventions.
Five-bank one-review matched five-bank three-review repair at 7/12 while using two fewer reviewer calls per activated case, directly motivating a reviewer-count comparison.
The nine-answer oracle covered 9/12 cases versus 7/12 for five answers, yet nine-bank one-review still scored 7/12, leaving a two-case oracle-to-final gap.
Five-vote plurality scored 2/12 despite its 7/12 oracle, showing that candidate availability alone is insufficient.

Blind regeneration increased oracle coverage by two cases and generated two new correct answers, but scored only 3/12 and had 0.00 weakest-family accuracy; its plurality fusion failed to exploit the new coverage.

Sequential cross-examination previously scored 5/12 with four helpful and zero harmful interventions, so it remains the strongest lower-priority orthogonal reviewer organization supported by a positive correction balance.

proposals.hypothesis: Replicate the exact Iteration 14 mechanism and pair it with the retained nine-bank three-review champion. The shared nine-answer bank offers 9/12 oracle coverage, while one reviewer matched the best exact score with fewer calls; the paired result will test whether extra reviewers add signal or variance.
proposals.name: One-Review Nine-Bank Falsifying Repair Replication

proposals.hypothesis: Replicate the unchanged candidate-generation mechanism on a larger sealed panel. Its first run added two correct answers absent from the base bank and raised oracle coverage from 7/12 to 9/12, although plurality converted that gain poorly; replication separates a stable coverage benefit from panel noise.
proposals.name: Blind Diversified Regeneration Replication

proposals.hypothesis: A deterministic nine-vote control uses the already-required shared nine-answer bank without reviewer calls. It isolates how much of the broader bank's oracle gain is converted by raw consensus before attributing improvement to repair.
proposals.name: Nine-Vote Plurality Control

proposals.hypothesis: Sequential falsification previously produced four helpful and zero harmful interventions while scoring 5/12. Replication tests whether conditioning each reviewer on one predecessor preserves coherent correction better than independent parallel reviewers.
proposals.name: Sequential Five-Bank Cross-Examination Replication

proposals.hypothesis: Three disjoint committee delegates may suppress isolated answer noise before a single repairer acts, while committee-disagreement routing avoids unnecessary review. This is a lower-priority orthogonal aggregation test because earlier committee evidence was weak.
proposals.name: Committee-Delegate Single Repair

research_hypotheses: On a shared nine-answer bank, one falsifying repairer will match or outperform three independent repairers because additional reviewers currently add variance without improving the correction balance.
Blind diversified regeneration provides a real domain-neutral oracle-coverage gain, but augmented plurality is an inadequate conversion rule when newly correct answers are isolated.
Nine-vote plurality will reveal whether broader candidate coverage improves consensus at all, at no marginal bank cost relative to the retained nine-base champion.
Sequential cross-examination may make three review calls more coherent than parallel review by forcing explicit falsification of the immediately preceding solution.
Committee delegates may improve robustness only if local pluralities suppress idiosyncratic errors; the fresh panel should treat this as a stress test, not a favored repair variant.
document.risk_note: The 12-case Iteration 14 tie is noisy, and the 18-case panel will still have wide family-level uncertainty. Shared answer banks make strategy comparisons efficient but correlated. With the usual four retained mechanisms, adding the first two proposals costs at most 306 calls: 162 for a nine-answer bank and 144 for eight total reviewer/regenerator calls per case. Even selecting two three-review alternatives remains at most 342 calls, below the 500-call cap. No development result supports a claim of generality until a frozen mechanism transfers to untouched problem families.
